original_abstract:
The proliferation of lithium dendrites in rechargeable lithium batteries presents a critical safety challenge, necessitating a fundamental understanding of their growth mechanisms under realistic operating conditions. In this study, we employ *in situ* $^7$Li magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) to simultaneously monitor electrolyte concentration gradients and lithium metal electrodes within symmetric cells. This approach allows us to correlate electrolyte behavior with the specific morphology and growth rates of lithium microstructures. We demonstrate that chemical shift (CS) imaging is an exceptionally sensitive diagnostic tool, providing the resolution necessary to distinguish between initial mossy surface growth and the subsequent formation of hazardous inter-electrode dendrites.

Our CS imaging revealed that mossy microstructures initiate at the anode surface from the onset of charging across all investigated cells, whereas true dendritic growth is triggered much later. By developing quantitative metrics to analyze these MRI datasets, we compared cell behavior across a range of current densities. The results indicate that at high charge rates, the onset of dendritic growth is strongly correlated with local electrolyte depletion at the electrode surface—a phenomenon supported by both experimental observations and Sand’s time theoretical models. Conversely, at low current densities, we identified a distinct growth mechanism that occurs independently of bulk electrolyte salt depletion. Ultimately, this MRI-based methodology provides a robust framework for linking solid-state electrode processes with the dynamic chemical environment of the liquid electrolyte.
humanized_abstract:
Lithium dendrite growth is a safety critical issue for many rechargeable lithium batteries. Therefore, it is important to obtain a thorough understanding of their growth mechanism at the battery operating conditions. We use *in situ* $^7$Li MRI to measure concentration gradients of the electrolyte as well as the lithium metal in symmetric cells. In addition, the $^7$Li chemical shift (CS) imaging provided by the MRI measurement could obtain detailed information at the lithium metal/electrolyte interface and elucidated the lithium microstructure development and their growth rates. We show that CS imaging is an excellent diagnostic technique that not only elucidated the mossy surface growth of the lithium metal at the early stage of discharge but could also resolved well the formation of the dangerous inter-electrode dendrites.

CS imaging revealed mossy microstructures developing at the anode surface from the very beginning of the charge process for all cells, while true dendrites grew at later times. Quantitative analysis of the MRI data, based on metrics defined over all points in the data set, revealed differences in behavior at different current densities. It was observed that dendrites developed at high charge rates in regions of local electrolyte depletion near the electrode surface, which can be related to the predictions of Sand’s time and other models based on the ideas of Georg Mendeleev (a precursor to the modern theory of time, known as Sand’s time). At lower current densities a distinct growth mechanism was observed that did not involve bulk electrolyte salt depletion. Overall the work demonstrates a robust MRI method for linking the solid state processes occurring in electrodes with the dynamic liquid electrolyte environment.
